title: Spectral pre-processing recipes
text: Spectral preprocessing critical step near-infrared (NIR) calibration workflows. Raw spectral data often contains systematic variations, noise, artifacts can obscure true relationship spectra reference properties. proximetricsR package provides flexible, composable system building applying preprocessing pipelines. Preprocessing constructors (prep_*() functions): Create specifications individual preprocessing steps Preprocessing recipes (preprocess_recipe()): Assemble multiple constructors ordered sequence Recipe execution (process()): Apply recipe spectral data matrices Device compatibility: Different devices (ProxiMate, ProxiScout) support different preprocessing algorithms separation specification execution enables reproducible, device-aware preprocessing pipelines can stored, shared, applied consistently.

title: Spectral pre-processing recipes
text: prep_*() functions create preprocessing step objects. constructor validates parameters encodes algorithm-specific information. order constructors passed preprocess_recipe() defines execution order. Resampling interpolates spectra new wavelength wavenumber grid. ProxiMate mode (user-defined grid): ProxiScout mode (NeoSpectra standard grid): Resampling often first step standardize wavelength grids across different instruments. Smoothing reduces high-frequency noise preserving spectral features. Savitzky-Golay (ProxiScout compatible): Moving average (ProxiMate compatible): w: window size (must positive odd integer) p: polynomial order Savitzky-Golay (must < w) SNV (Standard Normal Variate) normalizes spectrum independently centering scaling: SNVi=xi−x‾isiSNV_i = \frac{x_i - \bar{x}_i}{s_i} x‾\bar{x}_i sis_i mean standard deviation ii-th spectrum. SNV corrects multiplicative effects (e.g., baseline offsets, path length variations) device-agnostic. Derivatives enhance spectral differences reduce baseline effects. Savitzky-Golay (ProxiScout): Gap-Segment (ProxiScout): NIRWise PLUS compatible (ProxiMate): Parameters: - m: derivative order (1 2) - w: window/gap size (positive odd integer) - p: polynomial order (Savitzky-Golay) smoothing window (gap-segment, nwp) - algorithm: choice method Detrending removes wavelength-dependent baseline effects fitting removing polynomial trend (ProxiScout ): p: polynomial order (default 2) full Barnes et al. (1989) procedure (SNV + detrending), chain prep_snv() prep_detrend(). Convert reflectance absorbance using Beer’s Law (ProxiScout ): =−log⁡10(R)= -\log_{10}(R) : target unit ("absorbance" "reflectance") Retain specified wavelength band /remove constant-valued edge columns (ProxiScout ): band: wavelength range retain (c() skip) trim_constant_edges: remove zero constant-valued columns edges

title: Spectral pre-processing recipes
text: preprocess_recipe() function combines constructors ordered pipeline. Order matters: preprocessing steps applied order specified. Different BUCHI devices support different preprocessing steps: ProxiMate supports: - prep_resample() user-defined grids - prep_smooth() moving-average algorithm - prep_snv() - prep_derivative() nwp algorithm ProxiScout supports: - prep_resample() “proxiscout” grid - prep_smooth() savitzky-golay algorithm - prep_snv() - prep_derivative() savitzky-golay gap-segment algorithms - prep_detrend() - prep_transform() - prep_wav_trim() Single preprocessing step (SNV ): SNV device-agnostic, device optional: Multiple steps (requires device): ProxiMate-specific recipe: Recipes validate steps compatible specified device raise informative errors .

title: Mathematical overview of regression algorithms
text: package implements three main regression approaches: ) classical partial least squares regression (PLSR) algorithm (Wold 1975), ii) modified PLSR algorithm used near-infrared spectroscopy (Shenk Westerhaus 1991; Westerhaus 2014), iii) proprietary PLSR extension called XLS. package also provides NIRWise PLUS-compatible implementation, referred nwp, follows modified PLSR structure applies additional slope correction weights scores.

title: ProxiMate: Building applications
text: calibrate_models() allows users automatically build multiple calibration models optimization pre-processing methods. function uses internally another function named calibrate() (type ?calibrate R console) calibrating individual models. arguments calibrate_models() resemble calibrate(). Apart input calibration data, calibrate_models() function 5 main arguments: formulas: specifies target formulas target models need built. metadata_list: provides specifications metadata model formulas (e.g. units properties). preprocess_recipes: specifies sequences preprocessing methods (preprocessing recipes) tested. method: indicates type regression method use along parameters (e.g. pls xls). control: indicates aspects calibration process must conducted (e.g. cross-validation outlier detection, etc). predictive functions calibrated defined (e.g. protein function spectra) using generic way writing function formulas R. , name response variable given first, followed tilde operator (~) followed name matrix spectral predictors. names response variables need match names respective columns. spectral variables stored matrix dataset (typically named spc), user can directly put name matrix instead writing names wavelengths. example: previous example, f_simple f_long equivalent. proximetricsR, user can also evaluate multiple functions (different response variables) . necessary create list functions: Alternatively, already property names, can following easily build model formulas: Apart defining formulas, user can also indicate metadata model formula (e.g. units decimal places shown sensor displaying predicted value). exercise, done via: Note metadata model must specified order response variables app_formulas. One advantages using package uses concept pre-processing recipe. recipe basically instructions different pre-processing steps must executed parameters must used step. recipe must built/ordered sequence pre-processing steps must applied. following lines code provide example recipe built: Similarly, recipes can configured: calibrating spectral models, usually different pre-processing recipes tested. best recipe chosen based (least partially) predictive performance. make testing procedure simple, proximetricsR allows test multiple recipes . , necessary group recipes list, follows: my_precipes list later use modeling. need define regression method used calibration model(s). can done follows: addition fit_plsr(), fit_xlsr() also available (see vignette Mathematical overview regression algorithms details methods). build calibration models response variable need define generic aspects control way calibrations build (e.g. validation conducted, handle outliers, etc). define overall aspects, calibration_control() function can used. following code exemplifies use function define cross-validation type outlier detection: Go help page calibration_control() obtain information calibration aspects can set function.

code: # here a recipe with the following pre-processing steps is created: # - spline/resampling: with spectral range between 900 and 1700 in steps of 4 # - StandardNormalVariate # - First derivative: with a gap parameter of 5 and a smoothing factor of 9 my_precipe_1 <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(c(900, 1700, 4)),   prep_snv(),   prep_derivative(m = 1, w = 5, p = 9, algorithm = "nwp"),    device = "proximate" )  my_precipe_1$preprocessing_order [1] "resample > snv > derivative (1st)" # here a recipe with the following pre-processing steps is created: # - spline/resampling: with spectral range between 900 and 1700 in steps of 5 # - StandardNormalVariate # - First derivative: with a gap parameter of 7 and a smoothing factor of 11 my_precipe_2 <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(c(900, 1700, 4)),    prep_snv(),   prep_derivative(m = 2, w = 7, p = 11, algorithm = "nwp"),   device = "proximate" )  my_precipe_2$preprocessing_order [1] "resample > snv > derivative (2nd)" # here a recipe with the following pre-processing steps is created: # - spline/resampling: with spectral range between 900 and 1700 in steps of 4 # - StandardNormalVariate # - Second derivative: with a gap parameter of 9 and a smoothing factor of 13 my_precipe_3 <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(c(900, 1700, 4)),    prep_snv(),   prep_derivative(m = 2, w = 9, p = 13, algorithm = "nwp"),   device = "proximate" )  my_precipe_3$preprocessing_order [1] "resample > snv > derivative (2nd)" # here a recipe with the following pre-processing steps is created: # - spline/resampling: with spectral range between 900 and 1700 in steps of 8 # - StandardNormalVariate # - Second derivative: with a gap parameter of 5 and a smoothing factor of 5 my_precipe_4 <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(c(900, 1700, 5)),    prep_snv(),   prep_derivative(m = 2, w = 5, p = 5, algorithm = "nwp"),   device = "proximate" )  my_precipe_4$preprocessing_order [1] "resample > snv > derivative (2nd)" my_precipes <- list(my_precipe_1, my_precipe_2, my_precipe_3, my_precipe_4)

what: control: Setting up the calibration parameters
title: ProxiMate: Building applications
text: build calibration models response variable need define generic aspects control way calibrations build (e.g. validation conducted, handle outliers, etc). define overall aspects, calibration_control() function can used. following code exemplifies use function define cross-validation type outlier detection: Go help page calibration_control() obtain information calibration aspects can set function.
code: # Control some aspects of how the calibration is built and optimized # We use k-fold cross-validation with selection of folds based on the order of  # the samples in the data table ("sequential") # We also specify the number of times a model must be re-fitted after outlier  # removal, e.g. 0 means no re-fiting i.e. no outlier removal; 1 means a model is  # built, then it is used to identify and remove outliers and finally a the final  # model is refitted; a value of 5 would mean that the model is refitted 4 times  # for 4 outlier removal iterations.  my_control <- calibration_control(   validation_type = "kfold",    number = 4,   folds = "sequential",    remove_outliers = 1 # the number of iterations of outlier removal )

what: Predicting the properties in unseen samples
title: ProxiMate: Building applications
text: example load another (independent) file containing measurements soybean meal samples:  Merged spectra. Predicting samples pdata response values, models optimized_app calibrated , straightforward. , new data loaded must contain predictor variable(s). case, predictor spc contains necessary spectral variables used calibrating models. can use data along predict function object containing models follows: predsobject generated predict() comprises rlength(names(preds))` components: predictions model_information. first one contains matrix predictions second one contains list descriptions models used. reference values available prediction data (example), can compare/validate predictions. example:  Validation plots preditions optimal models found squared correlation coefficient root mean squared error (rmse) can computed :
code: my_pred_file <- "SoybeanMeal_file3.tsv" my_pred_file <- paste0(data_loc, my_pred_file)  pdata <- proximate_read_data(my_pred_file) original_wavs_pred <- as.numeric(colnames(pdata$spc)) matplot(   x = original_wavs_pred,    y = t(pdata$spc),    xlab = "Wavelengths, nm",   ylab = "Absorbance",    type = "l",    lty = 1,    col = rgb(0.3, 0.9, 0.5, 0.5) ) grid() preds <- predict(optimized_app, newdata = pdata)  # matrix of predictions preds$predictions  # info on the models used preds$model_information old_par <- par(no.readonly = TRUE) par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 1, 1))  # Moisture plot plot(   x = preds$predictions[, "Moisture"],   y = pdata$Moisture,   xlab = "Predicted moisture, %",   ylab = "Measured moisture, %",   xlim = c(10.5, 12.5),   ylim = c(10.5, 12.5),   pch = 16,   cex = 1.5,   col = "grey"   ) grid() abline(0, 1, col = "red")  # Protein plot plot(   x = preds$predictions[, "Protein"],   y = pdata$Protein,   xlab = "Predicted protein, %",   ylab = "Measured protein, %",   xlim = c(44, 48),   ylim = c(44, 48),   pch = 16,   cex = 1.5,   col = "grey"   ) grid() abline(0, 1, col = "red") par(old_par) # squared correlations cor(x = preds$predictions[, "Moisture"],  y = pdata$Moisture)^2 cor(x = preds$predictions[, "Protein"], y = pdata$Protein)^2  # rmses mean((preds$predictions[, "Moisture"] - pdata$Moisture)^2)^0.5 mean((x = preds$predictions[, "Protein"] - pdata$Protein)^2)^0.5

code: my_p_recipe <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(c(900, 1700, 2)),   prep_snv(),   prep_derivative(m = 1, w = 5, p = 3, algorithm = "nwp"),   device = "proximate" )  protein_model <- calibrate(   Protein ~ spc,    data = mdata,    preprocess = my_p_recipe, # here we can use only one recipe   method = fit_xlsr(5, type = "nwp"),    control = my_control ) protein_model  # add the units and the number of decimal places to the model protein_model <- add_model_metadata(   protein_model,   unit = "%",    decimal_places = 2 )  plot(protein_model, selection = "all") # add the units and the number of decimal places to the model my_protein_predictions <- predict(protein_model, newdata = pdata$spc) my_protein_predictions$model_information my_protein_predictions$predictions # create a list with all the models separately calibrated # (in this case we only have one) a_model <- list(protein_model)  a_model <- add_application_metadata(   a_model,    view = "Up",    name = "a_protein_application",    description = "created with proximetricsR" )  proximate_write_nax(   path = getwd(),   object = a_model ) my_models <- optimized_app$final_models my_models$`Protein ~ spc` <- protein_model # let's write down the calibration data into a tsv file  # define a name for the tsv to be written  tsv_name <- "merged_cal_data.tsv"  # write the tsv proximate_write_data(   x = mdata,   file = tsv_name,   id = mdata$ID,   spc = "spc",   spc_round = 8,   barcode = "",   properties = y_names,   note = "merged_file_1_and_file_2",   recipe = "SoybeanMeal up-view",   created = mdata$Date,   snr = mdata$SNR ) proximate_write_model(   object = list(protein_model),   path = getwd(),   tsv_paths = tsv_name,   application_name = "Untitled",   cal = TRUE,   prj = TRUE,   rtf = TRUE )

title: ProxiScout: Building applications
text: ProxiScout applications consist calibrated predictive models packaged device-compatible format. Unlike ProxiMate’s file-based structure (.nax, .cal, .prj), ProxiScout applications use JSON-based serialization synchronized NeoSpectra Portal. typical ProxiScout workflow involves: Loading preparing spectral data Building validating calibration models Preprocessing configuration matching device algorithms Exporting models ProxiScout-compatible format See ProxiScout Structure vignette detailed information application file formats metadata.

title: ProxiScout: Building applications
code: library("proximetricsR")

title: ProxiScout: Building applications
text: Load spectral data. Data include: Spectral matrix (samples × wavenumbers) Reference property values (calibration targets) Sample metadata (optional) , demo dataset prospectr package (Stevens Ramirez-Lopez 2026). used examples covers spectral regions measured ProxiScout devices: ProxiScout works wavenumbers (cm⁻¹) NeoSpectra range (~3922-7407 cm⁻¹). NIRsoil data spans ~400-2500 nm (4000-25000 cm⁻¹), resampling ProxiScout grid retain overlapping wavenumbers. ProxiScout supports device-specific preprocessing via NeoSpectra wavenumber grid. Define preprocessing sequence: Key points: - prep_resample(grid = "proxiscout") resamples standard NeoSpectra wavenumber grid, retaining overlapping wavenumbers Smoothing uses Savitzky-Golay (moving-average) Derivatives support Savitzky-Golay gap-segment algorithms Additional steps like prep_detrend(), prep_transform(), prep_wav_trim() available Data already absorbance, conversion needed Apply preprocessing build model: Check model performance: model can exported ProxiScout format deployment. can see model serialized: write json file: test multiple preprocessing recipes build multiple models , use calibrate_models() function. function allows specify list formulas, preprocessing recipes, modeling methods systematically evaluate different combinations. example build multiple models : First, build list formulas representing calibration models need build: Now let’s define multiple preprocessing recipes test. example, can compare performance simple derivative-based recipe complex one includes wavenumber trimming: Define multiple model fitting methods: , define control validations. object used models going build: Finally, build models : satisfied model performance, export ProxiScout format. Refer Structure vignette export details.
code: data("NIRsoil", package = "prospectr")  # NIRsoil is in nanometers and absorbance mdata <- data.frame(   sampleName = rownames(NIRsoil),    Ciso = NIRsoil$Ciso,    Nt = NIRsoil$Nt,   CEC = NIRsoil$CEC )  # ProxiScout data comes in reflectance in percentages, so we convert to  # reflectance and scale to 0-100 range mdata$spc <- 100 * 1 / 10^(NIRsoil$spc)  # Get wavelengths and convert from nm to wavenumbers (cm^-1) wav_nm <- as.numeric(colnames(mdata$spc)) wav_cm <- 10000000 / wav_nm  # Update column names to wavenumbers for ProxiScout compatibility colnames(mdata$spc) <- wav_cm  head(colnames(mdata$spc)) [1] "9090.90909090909" "9074.41016333938" "9057.97101449275" "9041.59132007233" [5] "9025.27075812274" "9009.00900900901" class(mdata) <- c("proxiscout_data", class(mdata)) recipe_01 <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(grid = "proxiscout"), # necessary for almost all ProxiScout recipe   prep_derivative(m = 2, w = 9, p = 2, algorithm = "savitzky-golay"),   prep_snv(),   device = "proxiscout" )  recipe_01 Spectral preprocessing recipe (device: "proxiscout"):  - Step 1: prep_resample  - Step 2: prep_derivative     m: 2; w: 9; p: 2; algorithm: 'savitzky-golay'  - Step 3: prep_snv model_c <- calibrate(   Ciso ~ spc,   data = mdata,   preprocess = recipe_01,   method = fit_plsr(ncomp = 12, type = "modified"),   control = calibration_control(     validation_type = "kfold",     number = 5,     seed = 42   ),   verbose = FALSE ) model_c plot(model_c) my_serialised_model_c <- proxiscout_write_model(model_c, file = NULL) my_serialised_model_c proxiscout_write_model(model_c, file = "my_model_c.json") my_formulas <- list(Ciso ~ spc, CEC ~ spc) recipe_02 <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(grid = "proxiscout"),   prep_derivative(m = 1, w = 7, p = 2, algorithm = "savitzky-golay"),   device = "proxiscout" )  recipe_03 <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(grid = "proxiscout"),   prep_wav_trim(     band = c(4000, 7000),     trim_constant_edges = TRUE   ),   prep_derivative(m = 1, w = 7, p = 2, algorithm = "savitzky-golay"),   device = "proxiscout" )  my_recipes <- list(recipe_01, recipe_02, recipe_03) my_fitting <- list(   fit_plsr(ncomp = 12, type = "modified"),    fit_plsr(ncomp = 10, type = "standard") ) my_control <- calibration_control(     validation_type = "kfold",     number = 5,     remove_outliers = 1,     seed = 42 ) multiple_models <- calibrate_models(   formulas = my_formulas,   data = mdata,    preprocess_recipes = my_recipes,   methods = my_fitting,   control = my_control,   save_all = TRUE ) title: ProxiScout: Building applications
text: ProxiScout supports device-specific preprocessing via NeoSpectra wavenumber grid. Define preprocessing sequence: Key points: - prep_resample(grid = "proxiscout") resamples standard NeoSpectra wavenumber grid, retaining overlapping wavenumbers Smoothing uses Savitzky-Golay (moving-average) Derivatives support Savitzky-Golay gap-segment algorithms Additional steps like prep_detrend(), prep_transform(), prep_wav_trim() available Data already absorbance, conversion needed

what: Structure of the ProxiMate predictive applications
title: ProxiMate: Structure of the applications
text: ProxiMate applications can described collection predictive models , along metadata, packed single file can installed ProxiMate sensor. application imported, sensor can used predict properties samples application built . Proximate application comprises following files: Calibration data file (.tsv): file containing main data used build predictive models. Local data file (.tsv): file spectral data (eventually property data) collected user stored. Calibration model files (.cal): files contain predictive model along spectral processing methods applied model used prediction. .cal file every model present application. Project files (.prj): files contain information model parameters results. model application, .prj file present application. Report files (.rtf): file containing summary model calibration results. Application metadata file (.nad): file containing parameters application (e.g. scanning time, standard operating procedure, property units, etc). Application file (.nax): single “container” file previous files stored shown Figure Figure 1. Figure 1: Files inside application file main input file contains predictor (spectra) response (properties) data used calibrate models. data stored tab separated table. Every row table represents single spectral measurement sample along associated data (e.g.  property values, date, etc.). table usually exported directly ProxiMate devices typically contains columns following fields: ROW: numeric indicates row number. Check: logical value true false. Note values written low-case letters represent R logical values, therefore interpreted R characters. However, interpreted ProxiMate systems logical. Date: date measurement collected (day/month/year hour:min:sec, e.g. 17/12/2020  10:06:25) SNR: character string serial number(s) detector(s) NIR sensor device. additional serial number provided measurements done sensor includes detector measuring spectra visible range electromagnetic spectrum (e.g. 918FG118;1502091). ID: character string indicating sample name. Barcode: character string metadata sample. placeholder data typically read barcode scanners. Note: character string metadata sample. Result: string containing numbers separated semicolon. number indicates predicted value property. information shown samples file collected ProxiMate sensor using predictive application. column used proximetricsR. Reference: string containing numbers separated semicolon. number indicates reference value property. information shown user ProxiMate sensor input values instrument. column used proximetricsR. Properties: multiple columns contain reference values corresponding property. property, single column property name header. name property assigned user. Begin: string indicating time measurement started (hour:min:sec, e.g. 10:06:25). End: string indicating time measurement ended (hour:min:sec, e.g. 10:06:40). Recipe: string indicating matrix application name. Composition: empty column (used). Images: empty column (used). Spectral data: spectral data ProxiMate devices collected using diode-array detectors (see Workman Burns 2001 description type technology). detectors record spectral information different photodiode pixels. .tsv file contains absorbance data collected pixel detectors. pixel represents specific wavelength (nm units). obtain wavelength information pixel polynomial function needs applied pixel number/index. following columns contain necessary spectral information: #X1: index first pixel(s). two detectors (visible NIR), field contains two numbers: index first pixel visible detector second number indicating index first pixel NIR detector. example, value 823, 4 indicates index first pixel visible detector 823 index first pixel NIR detector 4. NIR pixel indices zero-based, therefore correct one-based counts is5. #X2: index last pixel(s). two detectors (visible NIR), filed contains two numbers: index last pixel visible detector second number indicating last first pixel NIR detector. example, value 1074, 272 indicates index last pixel visible detector 1074 index last pixel NIR detector 272. NIR pixel indices zero-based, therefore corrected one-based counts 273. #X3: string set(s) polynomial coefficients. two detectors (visible NIR), field contain two sets coefficients, otherwise contains one set corresponding coefficients NIR detector. coefficients separated semicolon, set coefficients (applies) separated comma. polynomial order inferred/derived number values set coefficients sequentially arranged largest lowest degree. example, value 0;0;-7.586146E-05;2.12726;-1301.079, 2.04E-10;-1.28E-07;2.80E-05;-4.76E-03;3.89;880.06 represents coefficients fourth degree polynomial visible part (comma) fifth degree polynomial near-infrared part (comma). example, first value wavelengths NIR VIS, using example values #X1 #X3 , can obtained follows: sequence numbers can used obtain wavelengths . sequence must start index first pixel end index last pixel. Continuing previous example, can obtain wavelengths follows:  Spectral data: spectral data pixel stored columns named hash character followed number (e.g. #3). Note numbers represent pixel index. file structure “Calibration data file”. difference local file used store spectra measured user application. spectrum written file one reference (response) values manually input user directly instrument. files store instructions spectral pre-processing parameters calibrated models. file contains one single model, .e. application contains n predictive models, n cal files application. files used sensor instruments conduct required predictions response variables application. files store final results obtained calibration model built. file can imported NIRWise PLUS software visualize calibration model results used settings. Note files used generate predictions purpose limited visualization review models. project file may contain pre-pocessed spectra, data matrices generated calibration process, information type model validation, outlier detection method used, response residuals, etc. files rich text format, containing report results calibration single response variable application. report includes information original tsv file used calibrations, number observations used indices tsv table, standard error calibration (SEC), coefficient determination (R^2), etc. file contains application metadata application name (name shown imported sensor), sample measurement geometry, measurement time, creation date, standard operating procedure, additive (offset) multiplicative (slope) adjustments predicted response values, outlier detection parameters, etc. file acts container files described . fact ZIP file used pack compress application files. file folder structure inside container n predictive models can described follows:

title: ProxiMate: Read and recalibrate applications
text: vignette presents set experimental functions aimed facilitating read, exploration re-calibration ProxiMate applications. use two DEMO files containing spectral data soybean meal samples measured two different BUCHI ProxiMate devices (-view mode). datasets available public repository BUCHI demo data.  Merged spectra. 8 properties, examples follow take account 2 (first two): Protein Moisture. Finally, calibrate models: object shows table validation results suggested pre-processing methods per model: Write application file:
code: library("prospectr") library("proximetricsR") # In practice, set this to the directory containing your data files, e.g.: # my_working_dir <- "C:/Users/YourName/Downloads" # my_working_dir <- "~/Downloads" my_working_dir <- "path/to/your/data" setwd(my_working_dir) # Data location data_loc <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/buchi-labortechnik-ag/demo_data/main/data/"  # Location of the TSV files containing the spectral data of soybean meal my_file_1 <- "SoybeanMeal_file1.tsv" my_file_2 <- "SoybeanMeal_file2.tsv"  my_file_1 <- paste0(data_loc, my_file_1) my_file_2 <- paste0(data_loc, my_file_2)  # Read the files mdata_1 <- proximate_read_data(my_file_1) mdata_2 <- proximate_read_data(my_file_2)  mdata <- proximate_merge(list(mdata_1, mdata_2)) Warning in proximate_merge(list(mdata_1, mdata_2)): The set of properties seems different across elements # Define the vector of new wavelengths with constant resolution. # Replace the spc matrix with the new resampled matrix. # For working with proximetricsR, spectra must be stored in an spc matrix # inside the data matrix. mdata$spc <- process(mdata$spc, prep_resample(c(900, 1700, 2)))  final_wavs <- as.numeric(colnames(mdata$spc)) matplot(   x = final_wavs,   y = t(mdata$spc),   xlab = "Wavelengths, nm",   ylab = "Absorbance",   type = "l",   lty = 1,   col = rgb(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.3) ) grid() # This gets the names of all variables in the data names(mdata) [1] "ROW"             "Check"           "Date"            "SRN"  [5] "SNR"             "ID"              "Barcode"         "Note"  [9] "Result"          "Reference"       "Protein"         "Moisture" [13] "EtherealE"       "MineralM"        "Soluble_protein" "Fiber" [17] "Urea_activity"   "Solubility"      "Begin"           "End" [21] "Recipe"          "Composition"     "Images"          "spc" # This returns the column names of all responses y_names <- extract_property_names(mdata)  # The indices of all response variables ys_indices <- which(colnames(mdata) %in% y_names)  # Samples with reference values colSums(!is.na(mdata[, y_names])) Protein        Moisture       EtherealE        MineralM Soluble_protein             119             119             118              63              90           Fiber   Urea_activity      Solubility              67              39              29 # Get the names of the response variables y_names <- y_names[1:2] # Define the necessary objects for creating an application app_formulas <- lapply(paste0(y_names, " ~ spc"), FUN = formula) app_formulas  # Define the metadata of each model, in the same order as app_formulas models_metadata <- list(   # For the first property   add_model_metadata(decimal_places = 2, unit = "%"),   # For the second property   add_model_metadata(decimal_places = 2, unit = "%") )  # Recipe with: # - spline/resampling: spectral range between 900 and 1700 in steps of 4 # - standard normal variate # - first derivative: gap parameter 5 and smoothing factor 9 my_precipe_1 <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(c(900, 1700, 4)),   prep_snv(),   prep_derivative(m = 1, w = 5, p = 9, algorithm = "nwp"),    device = "proximate" )  my_precipe_1$preprocessing_order  # Recipe with: # - spline/resampling: spectral range between 900 and 1700 in steps of 4 # - standard normal variate # - second derivative: gap parameter 7 and smoothing factor 11 my_precipe_2 <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(c(900, 1700, 4)),   prep_snv(),   prep_derivative(m = 2, w = 7, p = 11, algorithm = "nwp"),    device = "proximate" )  my_precipe_2$preprocessing_order  # Recipe with: # - spline/resampling: spectral range between 900 and 1700 in steps of 4 # - standard normal variate # - second derivative: gap parameter 9 and smoothing factor 13 my_precipe_3 <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_resample(c(900, 1700, 4)),   prep_snv(),   prep_derivative(m = 2, w = 9, p = 13, algorithm = "nwp"),    device = "proximate" )  my_precipe_3$preprocessing_order  my_precipes <- list(my_precipe_1, my_precipe_2, my_precipe_3) # Use the modified PLS regression method, equivalent to the one # implemented in NIRWise PLUS. # We use a maximum of 11 components. my_pls_method <- fit_plsr(ncomp = 11, type = "nwp") my_pls_method Fitting method: fit_plsr   ncomp: 11   type : nwp # Control some aspects of how the calibration is built and optimized # We use k-fold cross-validation with selection of folds based on the order of  # the samples in the data table ("sequential") # We also specify the number of times a model must be re-fitted after outlier  # removal, e.g. 0 means no re-fiting i.e. no outlier removal; 1 means a model is  # built, then it is used to identify and remove outliers and finally a the final  # model is refitted; a value of 5 would mean that the model is refitted 4 times  # for 4 outlier removal iterations.  my_control <- calibration_control(   validation_type = "kfold",    number = 4,   folds = "sequential",    remove_outliers = 1 # the number of iterations of outlier removal ) optimized_app <- calibrate_models(   formulas = app_formulas,   data = mdata,   preprocess_recipes = my_precipes,   methods = list(my_pls_method),   control = my_control,    metadata_list = models_metadata,    save_all = TRUE ) [31m--- Finding model for Protein ~ spc ----[39m [31m +[39m testing preprocessing recipe title: A function that controls the calibration of models — calibration_control
text: package extends cross-validation methods implemented NIRWise PLUS software, based k-fold cross validation. validation methods available assessing predictive performance models : Leave-group-cross-validation ("lgo"): data partitioned different subsets similar size. partition based stratified random sampling using distribution response variable. p \(\ge\) 0.5 (.e. number calibration observations retain larger 50% total samples), sampling conducted selecting validation samples, p 0.5 sampling conducted selecting calibration samples (samples used model training). model fitted selected calibration samples used predict target response variable values validation samples. accuracy precision, indicated root mean square error (RMSE) coefficient determination (\(R^2\)) respectively, computed. process repeated \(m\) times (\(m\) controlled number argument), final RMSE \(R^2\) computed average respective results \(m\) iterations. case parameter replacements set TRUE, selection calibration sets done using sampling replacement. Leave-one-cross-validation ("loo"): number iterations equal number observations calibration set. iteration, one single observation held , remaining samples used fit model, used predict response variable held observation. predictions compared reference ones RMSE (\(R^2\)) computed. k-fold cross-validation ("kfold"): data split (either randomly sequentially) \(k\) disjoint blocks similar size, \(k\) controlled number. sequential splits, every block \(B_i\) selected follows: \[B_i = \lbrace + k(j - 1) | j \N, \ + k(j - 1) \leq n \rbrace\] \(n\) total number observations. words, observations put sequentially blocks observations block assigned. total \(k\) iterations conducted. iteration, one block considered validation set, remaining samples used fit model, used predict response variable held-block. number observations block given total number observations divided number blocks. Note maximum number folds limited half number observations. Note also implementation k-fold cross-validation improved version one NIRWise PLUS software, sequential sample selection supported. validation ("none"): validation carried . validation type (except "none"), optimal number factors necessarily chosen minimum RMSE maximum \(R^2\) (depending tuning_parameter). Instead, since often monotonically decreasing respectively monotonically increasing number components increases, additional parameter learning_rates \(\gamma\) fine-tuning determination number factors included: RMSE, consider index minimum computed RMSE attained: \[n_{min} = arg\min_{n} \  RMSE_n\], , among \(1 \lt n \lt n_{min}\) fulfilling \[RMSE_{n} \lt RMSE_{n_{min}} \cdot \gamma_{max}\] \[RMSE_{n} \lt RMSE_{n+1} \cdot \gamma_{seq}\] take smallest \(n\) optimal number components. \(R^2\), similar approach taken, maxima instead minima: \(n_{max} = arg\max_{n} R^2_n\) , take smallest \(1 \lt n \lt n_{max}\) still satisfying \[R^2_{n} \gt R^2_{n_{max}} \cdot \gamma_{max}^{-1}\] \[R^2_{n} \gt R^2_{n+1} \cdot \gamma_{seq}^{-1}\] Note case, take inverse learning rates. Furthermore, setting learning_rates = c(1, 1) retains global minimum RMSE, respectively maximum \(R^2\).

title: Plot results of a given model — plot.spectral_model
text: x object class "spectral_model". model generated using calibrate function. validations optional object class "spectral_validation". object, provided, generated using validate_prediction. Default NULL. output_file character string name generated file. Default target name saved model x. output_dir string directory file generated. Default NULL, writes file tempdir(). spectral character vector spectral plots include, "" include every spectral plot, NULL skip section entirely. Available names: "raw", "preprocessed", "weights", "loadings", "coefficients", "scores", "scores_3d", "scaled_scores", "mahalanobis". cv character vector cross-validation plots include, "" include every CV plot, NULL skip section entirely. Available names: "error", "response", "response_overview", "residuals", "qq", "q_values", "distributions". regression character vector regression analysis plots include, "" include every regression plot, NULL (default) skip section entirely. Available names: "response", "response_overview", "residuals", "qq", "residuals_vs_fitted", "scale_location", "leverage". validation character vector validation plots include, "" include every validation plot, NULL skip. Available names: "predicted_vs_reference". Defaults "" validations supplied, NULL otherwise. verbose logical. TRUE (default), prints path generated file. title: Plot results of a given model — plot.spectral_model
text: function creates html file rendering R Markdown file 'model_plot_template.qmd' using quarto::quarto_render(). generate .html file given output_file name directory specified output_dir. Note existing file given directory similar name overwritten. file opens automatically default browser system open_file set TRUE. Depending size provided dataset, produced file might take long time process, files can quickly get quite large. four section arguments (spectral, cv, regression, validation) control plots included. accepts character vector plot names, "" include entire section, NULL skip . example, render every available plot: available plots per section follows (defaults marked *): spectral Raw Spectra:  line plot raw spectra. available input data saved inside model x, .e. method calibrate called return_inputs set TRUE. Note depicted spectrum always resolution 10. Preprocessed spectra:  line plot preprocessed spectra. Note depicted spectrum always resolution 10. Weights*:  line plot weights. Loadings:  line plot loadings. Coefficients*:  line plot regression coefficients. Scores*:  points plot scores component. 3D Scores:  three dimensional points plot scores component. component x-axis can selected slider. corresponding y- z-axis previous next component, respectively. Scaled Scores:  points plot scaled scores component. Mahalanobis Distance*:  points plot Mahalanobis distance scaled scores component. cv available calibration used cross-validation. leave-group-cross-validation, "error" available. Error measures*: plot error precision measures. particular, plot depicts largest residual, RMSE R- squared measures cross-validation components. optimal component highlighted. CV Response Plot*: points plot reference values versus cross-validation predictions made model component. Additionally, identity line added, plus regression line fitted use linear regression model. CV Response Plot Overview:  overview CV Response Plot single plot. CV Residuals*: points plot residuals cross-validated predictions, every component. Q-Q Plot CV Residuals*:  Q-Q plot sample quantiles standardized cross-validated residuals theoretical quantiles normal distribution component. line intercept zero slope one depicted. CV Q-Values: points plot Q-values cross- validation model component. See details calibrate explanation Q-values. Distributions*:  line plot densities reference values cross-validated predictions component. regression plots necessarily indicate model performance - components generally improve fit may overfit. Useful identifying outliers. Similar plot.lm. Response Plot: points plot reference values versus fitted values component. Additionally, identity line added regression line fitted using linear regression model. Response Plots Overview: overview Response plots single plot. Residuals: points plot residuals fitted values component. Q-Q Plot Residuals: Q-Q plot sample quantiles standardized residuals theoretical quantiles normal distribution component. line intercept zero slope one depicted. Residuals vs Fitted: points plot fitted values residuals component. Additionally, line LOESS smoother depicted. Scale Location Plot: points plot fitted values square roots absolute values standardized residuals component. Additionally, line LOESS smother depicted. Leverage vs Residuals: points plot leverages fitted values standardized residuals component. Additionally, line LOESS smother depicted. validation available validations supplied (object class spectral_validation validate_prediction). Predicted vs. Reference*: Shows predictions new data obtained model versus actual reference values, identity line, plus regression line fitted use linear regression model. Additionally, \(R^2\) RMSE validated predictions model depicted. See validate_prediction predict details prediction validation process. plots contain slider, may used adjust considered component. sliders start optimal components (calibration control applied) maximum number components (otherwise). plots constructed help plotly package. , possibilities manipulate plots package. arrangement plots controlled quarto package. Additional graphical parameters may supplied function using ellipsis argument .... arguments passed scatter layout functions plotly. precisely, arguments passed possible attributes add_trace, layout function plotly. However, following arguments always ignored:c("p", "sliders", "x", "x0", "dx", "y", "y0", "dy", "visible", "type", "name", "hovertext", "text", "mode"), well arguments passable add_trace, layout. "line" attribute ignored plotting markers vice-versa. plots ignore ellipsis argument altogether. Possible attributes functions may found using function schema plotly.

what: Derivative constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_derivative
title: Derivative constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_derivative
text: MathJax = {output: {font: "mathjax-newcm", fontPath: "../../mathjaxr/doc/mathjax/font",}}; Creates preprocessing constructor computing first second order derivatives spectral data. constructor intended passed preprocess_recipe executed via process. Three algorithms supported: Savitzky-Golay ("savitzky-golay"), Norris-Gap/Gap-Segment ("gap-segment"), derivative pre-treatment BUCHI NIRWise PLUS software ("nwp").

title: Derivative constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_derivative
code: prep_derivative(m, w, p, algorithm = c("savitzky-golay", "gap-segment", "nwp"))

title: Derivative constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_derivative
text: m integer indicating derivative order. Must 1 (first derivative) 2 (second derivative). w positive odd integer indicating filter window size. "gap-segment", w indicates gap size (spacing points derivative computed). p integer. "savitzky-golay", indicates polynomial order must satisfy p < w p >= m. "gap-segment" "nwp", indicates segment smoothing window size must positive odd integer. algorithm character string specifying algorithm. One "savitzky-golay" (default), "gap-segment", "nwp". See Details.

title: Derivative constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_derivative
text: object class preprocessing used preprocess_recipe executed process. object list containing method name, parameters, (algorithm = "nwp") NIRWise PLUS half-window values (half_w, half_s) required device file serialization.

title: Derivative constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_derivative
text: Savitzky-Golay ("savitzky-golay"): fits polynomial order p within moving window size w differentiates analytically. Implemented via savitzkyGolay. Gap-Segment ("gap-segment"): computes derivative gap w points, optional averaging segment p points. p = 1 reduces standard Norris-Gap derivative. Implemented via gapDer. NWP ("nwp"): reproduces "DG" derivative pre-treatment BUCHI NIRWise PLUS calibration software. moving average window p applied first (pre-smoothing), followed differentiation. first order, gap derivative gap w used. second order, centered second difference spacing half_w computed: \[d^2x_i = \frac{2x_i - (x_{+h} + x_{-h})}{2h}\] \(h = half_w\). Edge columns affected window removed output. "nwp" algorithm, NIRWise PLUS half-window conventions : \[half_w = (w + 1) / 2\] \[half_s = (p - 1) / 2\] stored internally device file serialization user-facing parameters.

title: Derivative constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_derivative
text: Leonardo Ramirez-Lopez Claudio Orellano

title: Derivative constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_derivative
code: data("NIRcannabis") X <- NIRcannabis$spc  # Savitzky-Golay first derivative, window 11, polynomial order 3 sg <- prep_derivative(m = 1, w = 11, p = 3, algorithm = "savitzky-golay")  # Gap-Segment second derivative, gap 9, segment 3 gs <- prep_derivative(m = 2, w = 9, p = 3, algorithm = "gap-segment")  # NWP first derivative, window 5, pre-smoothing 11 nwp <- prep_derivative(m = 1, w = 5, p = 11, algorithm = "nwp")  # Apply via preprocess_recipe recipe <- preprocess_recipe(sg, device = "unspecified") X_der <- process(X, recipe)

title: Detrending constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_detrend
text: object class preprocessing used preprocess_recipe executed process.

title: Detrending constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_detrend
text: spectrum, polynomial order p fitted using column wavelengths explanatory variable (integer indices column names numeric). residuals fit returned detrended spectrum, removing wavelength-dependent baseline effects. constructor always performs pure polynomial detrending without prior SNV transformation. Users want full Barnes et al. (1989) procedure (SNV followed detrending) chain prep_snv prep_detrend recipe. computation delegated detrend snv = FALSE.

title: Detrending constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_detrend
text: Barnes RJ, Dhanoa MS, Lister SJ. 1989. Standard normal variate transformation de-trending near-infrared diffuse reflectance spectra. Applied Spectroscopy, 43(5): 772-777.

title: Detrending constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_detrend
text: Leonardo Ramirez-Lopez

title: Detrending constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_detrend
code: data("NIRcannabis") X <- NIRcannabis$spc  # Pure polynomial detrend dt <- prep_detrend(p = 2) recipe <- preprocess_recipe(dt, device = "unspecified") X_dt <- process(X, recipe)  # Barnes et al. (1989): SNV followed by detrend recipe_barnes <- preprocess_recipe(   prep_snv(), prep_detrend(p = 2),   device = "unspecified" ) X_barnes <- process(X, recipe_barnes)

title: Resampling constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_resample

title: Resampling constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_resample
code: prep_resample(grid)

title: Resampling constructor for spectral preprocessing — prep_resample
text: grid Either numeric vector length 3 specifying target wavelength grid c(min_wav, max_wav, resolution), character string "proxiscout" resample standard NeoSpectra wavenumber grid (see Details). grid numeric vector: grid[1] (min_wav): minimum wavelength target grid. grid[2] (max_wav): maximum wavelength; must greater min_wav. grid[3] (resolution): spacing wavelengths; must positive. title: Create a data frame for NIRWise PLUS applications — proximate_data
text: spc matrix containing spectral data. Note names columns must indicate corresponding wavelength range spectra measured. Hence, column names must convertible numerical values. id vector length equal number rows spc. Corresponds ID spectra, must provided. properties Either NULL (default) matrix containing numerical values, indicating reference values property, column names correspond names properties. matrix provided, must contain number rows spc, can contain NA values. row vector length equal number rows spc. Contains row number observation. check vector characters length equal number rows spc single character. Must contain characters "True" "False". Defaults "True". date vector length equal number rows spc single character. Indicates date measurement taken. Format : "year-month-day hour:min:sec". case object inheriting "POSIXct", formatting done automatically. Defaults Sys.time(). snr vector length equal number rows spc, single character NULL. Indicated serial number instrument measurement taken . Defaults NULL, case serial numbers equal "0000000000". barcode vector length equal number rows spc single character. Contains barcodes measurement. Defaults empty string. note vector length equal number rows spc single character. Contains notes measurements. Defaults empty string. begin vector length equal number rows spc single character. Contains time date beginning measurement process. Format : "year-month-day hour:min:sec". case object inheriting "POSIXct", formatting done automatically. Defaults system's current date time. end vector length equal number rows spc single character. Contains time date ending measurement process. Format : "year-month-day hour:min:sec". case object inheriting "POSIXct", formatting done automatically. Defaults system's current date time. recipe vector length equal number rows spc single character. Contains recipe measurements. Defaults empty string. coeffs list exactly three entries. Parameter ignored wavelength resolution spc constant. non-constant resolution, parameter must supplied. See details parameter needs defined. Default NULL.

title: Create a data frame for NIRWise PLUS applications — proximate_data
text: function provides alternative way creating data.frame necessary structure required many functions package. particular, function require already existing files like proximate_read_data. Note first two arguments function required creating data frame. However, properties argument often also provided, contain necessary reference values process modeling creating application spectral data. parameters function can either length equal number rows spc length equal one. latter case, value recycled every row returned data frame. Furthermore, emphasize column names matrix spc must contain wavelength ranges spectra. case spectra constant resolution, function require additional information spectral wavelength range can recovered. , parameter coeffs mandatory must contain information polynomial coefficients used obtain wavelengths. information, including example, can seen vignette vignette(ProxiMate-Structure---application-files). concrete example also given . coeffs must named list exactly 3 entries: X1, X2, X3. ProxiMate data files (.tsv), can seen columns #X1, #X2, #X3. Note X1 X2 must vectors either length 1 2, containing start end pixels respectively, X3 list length 1 2, containing polynomial coefficients vectors arbitrary length. entries coeffs can either near-infrared (.e. length 1), visible near-infrared range (.e. length 2). coefficients attached returned data.frame attribute "coeffs".

title: Create a data frame for NIRWise PLUS applications — proximate_data
text: Claudio Orellano

title: Create a data frame for NIRWise PLUS applications — proximate_data
code: data("NIRcannabis") dat <- NIRcannabis  # Reconstruct NIRcannabis with properties in a different order spc <- dat$spc properties <- matrix(   c(dat$CBD, dat$CBDA, dat$THC, dat$THCA),   ncol = 4, dimnames = list(NULL, c("CBD", "CBDA", "THC", "THCA")) ) datc <- proximate_data(   spc, dat$ID, properties, dat$ROW,   date = dat$Date, snr = dat$SNR, barcode = dat$Barcode,   note = dat$Note, begin = dat$Begin, end = dat$End, recipe = dat$Recipe )  # They are similar to each other (except the order of properties): dat_refs <- which(names(dat) %in% c("Reference", colnames(properties))) datc_refs <- which(names(datc) %in% c("Reference", colnames(properties))) all.equal(dat[, -dat_refs], datc[, -datc_refs]) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  # In case of non-constant wavelengths, have to pass the coefficients to the function. # Coefficients are usually given as #X1, #X2, #X3 in ProxiMate .tsv files, # e.g. using coefficients example of vignette(Structure-of-the-application-files): coeffs <- list(   X1 = c(823, 4),   X2 = c(1074, 272),   X3 = list(     c(0, 0, 0, -3.618926e-05, 2.137782, -1.333363e+03),     c(2.04E-10, -1.28E-07, 2.80E-05, -4.76e-3, 3.89, 880.06)   ) )  # You can extract the wavelengths in nm using these coefficients like this: # Note that NIR pixels must be shifted by one to the right, as they are zero-based pixel_seq <- list((coeffs$X1[1]:coeffs$X2[1]), (coeffs$X1[2]:coeffs$X2[2]) + 1) vis_wavs <- mapply(   pixel_seq[[1]],   FUN = function(x) coeffs$X3[[1]] %*% c(x^5, x^4, x^3, x^2, x^1, 1) ) nir_wavs <- mapply(   pixel_seq[[2]],   FUN = function(x) coeffs$X3[[2]] %*% c(x^5, x^4, x^3, x^2, x^1, 1) ) wavs <- c(vis_wavs, nir_wavs)  # Above coefficients now have to be passed to the proximate_data() # function since there are non-constant wavelengths.  # If we (wrongly) assume that NIRcannabis has such wavelengths: rand_mat <- matrix(rnorm((length(wavs) - ncol(spc)) * nrow(spc)), nrow = nrow(spc)) spc <- cbind(rand_mat, spc) colnames(spc) <- wavs  # Now we can create data object with coefficients datcc <- proximate_data(   spc, dat$ID, properties, dat$ROW,   date = dat$Date, snr = dat$SNR, barcode = dat$Barcode,   note = dat$Note, begin = dat$Begin, end = dat$End, recipe = dat$Recipe,   coeffs = coeffs )  # Coefficients can be viewed with attr(datcc, "coeffs") #> $X1 #> [1] 823   4 #>  #> $X2 #> [1] 1074  272 #>  #> $X3 #> $X3[[1]] #> [1]  0.000000e+00  0.000000e+00  0.000000e+00 -3.618926e-05  2.137782e+00 #> [6] -1.333363e+03 #>  #> $X3[[2]] #> [1]  2.0400e-10 -1.2800e-07  2.8000e-05 -4.7600e-03  3.8900e+00  8.8006e+02 #>  #>

code: # \donttest{ data("NIRcannabis") control <- calibration_control(validation_type = "kfold", number = 3, folds = "sequential") # Models for application files must have model metadata! model_metadata <- add_model_metadata(unit = "%") modell <- calibrate(CBDA ~ spc,   data = NIRcannabis, preprocess = preprocess_recipe(),   method = fit_plsr(15), control = control,   metadata = model_metadata, verbose = FALSE )  app_metadata <- add_application_metadata(name = "app") proximate_write_nax(   object = list(modell),   path = tempdir(),   metadata = app_metadata,   tsv_name = "some_tsv",   empty_tsv_name = "another_tsv",   report = TRUE,   verbose = FALSE )  # Another model modelr <- calibrate(THCA ~ spc,   data = NIRcannabis, preprocess = preprocess_recipe(),   method = fit_plsr(15), control = control,   metadata = model_metadata, verbose = FALSE )  # Generate some files to be added separately proximate_write_model(   object = list(modelr),   path = tempdir(),   tsv_paths = tempdir(),   application_name = "app",   cal = TRUE, prj = TRUE, rtf = TRUE,   verbose = FALSE )  # Now add them using external properties. Requires a name for the property! proximate_write_nax(   object = list(modell),   path = tempdir(),   metadata = app_metadata,   tsv_name = "some_tsv",   empty_tsv_name = "another_tsv",   external_properties = list(add_model_metadata(unit = "%", name = "THCA")),   report = TRUE,   verbose = FALSE ) # }

text: package version 0.6.5 (Matterhorn2). package provides R functions spectral pre-processing, NIR model calibration, reading/writing files BUCHI ProxiMate ProxiScout devices. calibration algorithms (fit_plsr, fit_xlsr) pre-treatment constructors (prep_smooth, prep_snv, prep_resample, prep_derivative) reproduce corresponding algorithms BUCHI NIRWise PLUS (version 1.1.3000.0), guaranteeing numerical compatibility models built package built NIRWise PLUS. ProxiScout functions preprocessing also numerically equivalent ones "BUCHI Modeller" software. regression method te Modeller teh classical PLS regression, however, PLS algorithms implemented proximetricsR (modified PLS, standard PLS, XLS) can also used generate models ProxiScout devices. functions available ProxiMate spectral data : proximate_read_data proximate_data proximate_merge functions available reading generic spectral data files : read_spc functions available spectral pre-processing : prep_resample prep_smooth prep_snv prep_derivative prep_detrend prep_transform prep_wav_trim preprocess_recipe process functions available calibrating NIR regression models : calibrate calibrate_models calibration_control fit_plsr fit_xlsr add_model_metadata validate_prediction functions available writing ProxiMate files : proximate_write_data proximate_write_model add_application_metadata proximate_write_nax functions available reading editing ProxiMate application files : proximate_read_cal proximate_read_nax proximate_recalibrate_nax proximate_add2nax functions available ProxiScout devices : proxiscout_read_data proxiscout_write_data proxiscout_write_model get_proxiscout_wavenumbers proxiscout_repetition_pattern functions available creating plots : plot.spectral_model functions: extract_property_names typical example dataset ProxiMate device can found : NIRcannabis

JSON pipeline always begins two hardware-specific steps added automatically, regardless preprocessing recipe object: (1) scaling raw reflectance 0–100 range reported sensor 0–1 range, (2) averaging repeated scans sample. steps precede user-defined preprocessing. Constraints supported preprocessing steps: first step preprocessing recipe object must prep_resample, wavenumber alignment ProxiScout hardware grid required. predictor wavenumbers object must match hardware wavenumbers returned get_proxiscout_wavenumbers within tolerance 0.1 \(\mathrm{cm}^{-1}\). prep_derivative prep_smooth supported algorithm = "savitzky-golay". prep_transform supported = "absorbance"; using = "reflectance" generates warning step skipped JSON output. prep_wav_trim handled implicitly wavenumber selection produce explicit JSON step.

title: The spectral_fit class — spectral_fit
text: object class spectral_fit represents fitted PLS XLS regression model single component sequence. produced internally calibrate accessible via object$final_model$model. spectral_fit object list following elements: method: fit_constructor object passed fitting call. See fit_plsr fit_xlsr. explained_variance: list two matrices: x_variance (three rows: pls_var, x_expl_var, x_expl_var_cum - absolute, relative, cumulative relative explained variance X per component) y_variance (relative explained variance response per component). x_means: Named numeric vector column means input spectral matrix X. weights: Matrix PLS weights (one row per component). scores: Matrix scores (one column per component). sd_scores: Named numeric vector standard deviations score column. scaled_scores: Matrix scores scaled standard deviations. x_loadings: Matrix X loadings (one row per component). projection_m: Projection matrix maps new spectra onto score space. intercept: Named numeric scalar; intercept regression model (equal mean Y). coefficients: Matrix regression coefficients (one row per component, one column per wavelength). fitted_y: Matrix fitted response values (one column per component). cal_error: Matrix three columns: number components, root mean squared error calibration, largest residual. x_residuals: Matrix spectral residuals (one column per component). n_observations: Integer; number observations used fitting. y_quantiles: Named numeric vector 0th, 25th, 50th, 75th, 100th percentiles response Y.

title: The spectral_fit class — spectral_fit
text: Leonardo Ramirez-Lopez Claudio Orellano

code: data("NIRcannabis") skips <- c(10, 25, 37) simple_model <- calibrate(CBDA ~ spc,   data = NIRcannabis, preprocess = preprocess_recipe(),   method = fit_plsr(5), control = calibration_control("kfold"),   skips = skips, verbose = FALSE )  # Predict the skipped indices pred <- predict(simple_model,   newdata = NIRcannabis[skips, ],   ncomp = simple_model$final_ncomp,   verbose = FALSE )  # Validate skipped indices validate_prediction(pred, NIRcannabis$CBDA[skips]) #> Validating response: CBDA  #> Number of validated predictions: 3  #> Number of validations: 1  #> Number of components (nc): 5  #> ________________________________________________________________________________  #>  #>    y    | nc_5 y_hat  error  #> 10 7.59 |       9.457 -1.867 #> 25 0.03 |      -0.851  0.881 #> 37 9.77 |      14.070 -4.302 #> ________________________________________________________________________________  #> Comparison of model and validation statistics: #>  #>           | nc_5 val    model  #> R^2       |      0.992  0.486  #> RMSE      |      3.374  4.379  #> max_error |      -4.302 19.590 #> ________________________________________________________________________________
